title: Behind-the-Meter Natural Gas Power Plants: An Emerging Trend
text:
Recent work has given me the opportunity to dive deep into U.S. natural gas markets—supply, pipeline infrastructure, and emerging demand patterns.
What impressed me most? The wealth of information from EIA and industry sources. Pipeline operators and gas distributors openly discuss their growth strategies in investor presentations — for example a Kinder Morgan shipper meeting presentation1 and an EIA overview of natural gas supply trends2.
This article maps the emerging market for behind-the-meter (BTM) natural gas generation at data centers—a short-to-mid-term strategy gaining serious traction. The goal: understand market scale, key players, technology choices, and how renewables factor in amid gas price uncertainty.
Market Scale and Geography
Approximately 28 projects totaling ~55 GW are in development or operation across the U.S. (as of May 2026), with heavy concentration in Texas’s Permian Basin. This represents a significant shift in how large-scale electricity demand is being met outside traditional utility frameworks.
BTM approaches bypass the 5-10 year grid interconnection queues that have become a major bottleneck for new load. By connecting directly to gas infrastructure, these projects can be operational in 12-24 months. Data center developers are finding fast-track routes by partnering with natural gas companies eager to execute immediately.
Carbon Capture Still in Play
Eight projects incorporate carbon capture and storage (CCS) technology, targeting >90% CO₂ capture rates. The 45Q tax credits—available for projects initiated before 2033—continue to make CCS relevant. Post-combustion capture systems from providers like ExxonMobil, GE Vernova, and Shell are being integrated into project designs.
Technology and Integration
The technology mix is sophisticated: combined-cycle turbines, post-combustion CCS, and increasingly, hybrid approaches that integrate solar, wind, and battery storage alongside gas generation. This isn’t simply a return to fossil fuels—it’s a recognition that baseload power needs and grid constraints require flexible, immediately deployable solutions.
The Strategic Question
This convergence sidesteps electricity grid constraints while the gas industry expands into a lucrative new market segment. But the long-term trajectory remains uncertain: Will these sites eventually interconnect to the grid? Transition fully to renewables or hydrogen? Or remain as dedicated, off-grid generation?
The interplay between these pathways—and the economics that drive them—will shape both data center development and energy infrastructure investment for years to come.

title: The Future of U.S. Electricity Demand Through 2030
text: Over the past few months, I’ve been compiling a comprehensive dataset on electricity load projections across the United States as part of a larger research project on national electricity demand modeling that we hope to release later this year or early next year. While working on it, I wanted to benchmark our internal projections with public data across the U.S.—which led to this quick side project using a mix of domain knowledge and data extraction.

title: The Future of U.S. Electricity Demand Through 2030
text:
Understanding What’s Driving Growth
The NERC assessment map provides crucial context for understanding these regional variations.



Source: NERC Long-Term Reliability Assessment 20241



Data centers are a major factor across most regions, particularly in Texas, the Southeast, and PJM territory
Industrial electrification and large industrial loads are accelerating in manufacturing-heavy regions
Transportation electrification is contributing to baseline growth across all areas
Heat pumps and building electrification are reshaping winter peak dynamics in the Northeast and California


Looking Ahead
Regardless of the precise accuracy of these projections, they provide valuable insight into the scale of transformation our electric grid is undergoing. We’re not talking about incremental adjustments; we’re looking at a fundamental reshaping of electricity demand patterns that will require unprecedented levels of investment in generation, transmission, and grid flexibility.
For those working in energy markets and grid planning, understanding these regional dynamics will be crucial for navigating the next decade of change.


Important Caveats and Context
A few critical notes:
Scenario heterogeneity: The projections across regions are based on different scenarios and methodologies. ERCOT’s figure assumes a 62.6% load factor with a peak load of 148 GW, while SPP uses a “Moderate Electrification” scenario. This means we’re comparing somewhat different assumptions across regions—potentially apples to oranges in some cases.
Calculation uncertainties: Some values are derived from peak load projections and assumed load factors rather than direct annual energy forecasts, introducing additional uncertainty.
The relative stability of market share: Despite ERCOT and SPP’s dramatic percentage increases, their share of total US load grows more moderately—from 11% to 15% for ERCOT and from 7% to 9% for SPP. This indicates that the regional distribution of load is largely maintaining historical patterns.

title: Distribution Grid Analysis: A Primer
text:
Distribution grid planning tools simulate how power flows across feeders, transformers, and substations under different load, generation, and topology scenarios, giving utilities and regulators the technical basis for interconnection approvals and infrastructure investment decisions.
Commercial distribution planning tools are favored by utilities primarily due to their validated interfaces, regulatory acceptance in rate case filings, and dedicated vendor support. More recently, open source tools are increasingly used by academia and utilities and developers for DER integration studies due to their flexibility, python interoperability, and no licensing costs. The two ecosystems are not mutually exclusive and many utilities run commercial tools for core planning while using open source frameworks for scenario analysis, DER impact studies, and pilot evaluation. The interactive summary below provides details about the capabilities and use cases for open source distribution grid planning tools.

title: Distribution Grid Analysis: A Primer
text:
Study Types and Regulatory Triggers
Distribution interconnection analysis consists of seven study types. Applicable state tariff or federal standard as well as technology type and size determine the type of analysis that needs to be conducted for a specific interconnection.




  Regulatory Triggers by Study Type
  Which studies are required, when, and under what standard — across U.S. distribution interconnection frameworks

  
    By study type
    By technology
  

  
  
    All 7
    Triggered by interconnection
    Varies by state
    Utility judgment
    Engineering practice only
  

  








Study Type
Primary Regulatory Trigger
Governing Standard / Requirement





Load flow (QSTS) Triggered

DER interconnection application above simplified-process threshold; new large load; feeder reconfiguration.
State utility tariff; IEEE 1547-2018; ICA requirement in CA (CPUC Rule 21).



Hosting capacity analysis Varies by state

California: mandatory quarterly ICA update (Rule 21). Other states: voluntary or on-request. No national requirement.
CPUC Rule 21 (CA); IREC 2018 framework; LBNL IDSP components.



Fault / short-circuit analysis Triggered

New generation or storage interconnection above utility threshold; large load with on-site generation.
ANSI/IEEE C37 series; IEC 60909; IEEE 1547-2018 anti-islanding; NERC FAC-002.



Protection coordination Triggered

Any interconnection that changes fault current on a feeder above utility threshold.
Utility tariff; IEEE 1547-2018. ⚠ No national distribution standard for IBRs — gap identified by NERC 2025 



Harmonic analysis Utility judgment

Large load with significant power electronics (data center, EV charging hub); required if IEEE 519 compliance is at risk.
IEEE 519-2022. ⚠ No automatic MW threshold — triggered by utility engineer judgment 



Transformer thermal aging Engineering practice only

Not systematically required by any regulatory process. Utility engineering practice only.
IEEE C57.91 — an engineering standard, not a regulatory requirement.



Voltage sag analysis Engineering practice only

Included in fault analysis by commercial tools (CYMDIST). Not separately required by regulation.
IEEE 1564; IEEE 1159; SEMI F47 (industrial customers).





  
    Triggered Required when interconnection event occurs
    Varies Mandatory in some states only
    Judgment Utility discretion, no automatic threshold
    Practice Engineering convention, no regulatory requirement
  
  

  
  Each technology interconnects through its own process, under its own standard, studied independently. No existing framework requires assessing combined impacts on the same feeder.
  



Technology
Study triggered
Standard / tariff basis
Key gap




Rooftop solar / DER
Simplified or full interconnection study depending on size relative to feeder peak. Fast-track possible if within hosting capacity map limits.
IEEE 1547-2018; CPUC Rule 21 (CA); state-specific tariffs. ICA maps used as screen.
Hosting capacity maps may be stale. No study for cumulative DER below individual thresholds. Map methodology not standardized nationally.


Battery storage (BESS)
Interconnection study as combined generation and load; protection coordination required for bidirectional flow. Grid-forming BESS may require additional dynamic study.
IEEE 1547-2018; UL 9540; IEEE 2030.2. Bidirectional metering and protection standards vary by state.
No established national standard for grid-forming inverter protection coordination. Microgrid relay gap documented by NERC. Bidirectional hosting capacity not routinely calculated.


EV charging (EVSE)
Utility load study if installation exceeds existing site service capacity or threshold. No federal EVSE interconnection standard exists.
SAE J3400 / CHAdeMO (hardware); no national grid interconnection standard for EVSE. State utility tariffs only.
No mandatory interconnection study below MW threshold. Cumulative feeder impact from many EVSE is invisible to the study process. Fleet depot charging treated as single large customer.


Large load (>5–20 MW)
Large Load Interconnection Study (LLIS) or material change provision in existing tariff. Includes load flow, fault, harmonic, and protection studies.
State utility tariff (distribution); NERC FAC-002 (bulk); ERCOT PGRR115 Dec 2025 (>25 MW, TX); FERC Docket RM26-4-000 — action by June 2026 (>20 MW at transmission only).
FERC action will address transmission-level interconnection only. No national standard for distribution-level large load studies. Load shape changes not captured by MW-based triggers.

title: Distribution Grid Analysis: A Primer
text:
What Individual Studies Cannot Capture
Some utilities, particularly in states with integrated distribution planning requirements such as California and New York, conduct combined multi-technology feeder analysis internally. But no US regulatory framework requires it as a condition of interconnection approval.
This gap carries two compounding costs: reliability risk from inadequate combined modeling and non-wires alternatives not surfacing when they could be a lower cost solution. With respect to the former, NERC’s 2024 advisory linked 16 coordinated IBR disturbance events to poor multi-resource study practice.3 In the case of the latter, technologies studied independently cannot surface whether aggregated DER, storage, or demand response could defer a planned infrastructure upgrade. New York’s BQDM program illustrated the stakes: mandated multi-resource analysis identified a ~$200 million demand-side solution where wire-first planning had been tracking a $1 billion substation upgrade.4
The analysis below maps what compound technology interactions look like when co-located assets are assessed together.




Co-Located Technologies: Feeder Impacts, Regulatory Gaps, and Upgrade Decisions

  No US jurisdiction requires a combined multi-technology distribution study when DER, BESS, EV, and large loads accumulate on the same feeder over time. Each is studied individually when it interconnects. Compound stress emerges between study cycles.




  Three scenarios on the same industrial feeder
  
    
      Midday — best case (11am–2pm)
      Solar: generating
      BESS: charging from solar
      Large load: steady
      EV fleet: low charging
      Net feeder stress
      
      Solar partially offsets large load. BESS absorbs excess. Thermal loading moderate.
    
    
      Evening — worst case (5–9pm)
      Solar: offline
      BESS: may be depleted
      Large load: cooling peak
      EV fleet depot: peak charge
      Net feeder stress
      
      Full thermal loading. No generation offset. High probability of transformer violation.
    
    
      Post-outage restoration
      EV: simultaneous reconnect
      Large load: UPS retransfer
      BESS: anti-islanding response
      Solar: reconnect delay
      Cold load pickup surge
      
      Indianapolis AMI study (2020–2025): combined EV + large load surge is 46% more volatile than single-technology events.
    
  
  Four compound technical problems not captured by current planning tools
  
    1. Harmonic stackingIEEE 519-2022 limits apply per customer at the point of common coupling. Multiple customers, each individually compliant, can collectively exceed feeder harmonic limits. No regulatory mechanism detects the aggregate violation.
    2. Transformer aging accelerationA large load at 85% load factor keeps the transformer near rated temperature continuously. EV charging in the evening prevents overnight cooling. Combined thermal history substantially accelerates aging beyond either load in isolation — but no regulatory trigger captures this interaction.
    3. Protection coordination invalidationEach technology changes fault current characteristics. Incremental accumulation of multiple small-to-medium sources can invalidate protection coordination without any single source exceeding the threshold for a required protection study.
    4. Coincident demand uncertaintyStandard coincidence factors were calibrated on residential and commercial loads. Data centers, EV fleet depots, and rooftop solar have coincidence characteristics not captured in conventional planning models. (E3, December 2025)
  




  
    
      Decision flow when multiple technologies appear on a feeder
      
        Trigger: new interconnection request or material load changeEach technology studied individually. No combined study required. Utility runs power flow for that technology only.
        QSTS load flow — identify binding constraintDoes new load/generation cause: thermal overload? Voltage violation? Protection coordination failure? Harmonic limit breach?
        Screen for NWA eligibilityIs the constraint addressable by demand flexibility, smart inverter control, BESS dispatch, or managed charging? Required in NY, CA. Absent in most states.
        Cost-benefit comparisonCompare capital upgrade cost vs. NWA program cost + residual risk. IOU incentive typically favors capital under cost-of-service regulation.
        Decision: upgrade, NWA, or hybridUpgrade: reconductor, new transformer, new feeder, substation expansion. NWA: BESS, managed charging, DERMS, demand response, Volt/VAR optimization.
      
    
    
      NWA options by constraint type
      
        Thermal overload
        Managed EV chargingHigh effectiveness
        BESS peak shavingHigh effectiveness
        Demand response (large load)Emerging
        Dynamic line ratingGrid enhancing tech
      
      
        Voltage violation
        Smart inverter Volt/VARNo capex
        BESS reactive powerHigh effectiveness
        Capacitor bank resizingLow capex upgrade
      
      
        Protection coordination failure
        Adaptive relay settingsNWA (software)
        Anti-islanding upgradeRelay hardware
        ReconductoringHard upgrade required
      
      CA LMS study (Kevala 2025): peak reduction programs save $3.7–13.7B NPV in distribution capital costs by 2030. Highest-value NWA is demand flexibility on feeders with the largest overloads.
    
  




  
    
      Investor-owned utility (IOU)
      Serves ~68% of US customers via 6% of utilities
      Revenue model
      →Rate of return on rate base (~9.7–9.75% authorized ROE, 2025). Every approved capital dollar earns a regulated return permanently until retired from rate base.
      →CAPEX flows to rate base and earns return. OPEX (e.g., contracted DERMS, managed charging programs) does not earn return and may reduce earnings if it causes opex budget overruns.
      Incentive implications
      →Financial incentive differential between wires upgrades and NWAs. A $10M feeder reconductoring earns regulated return; a $10M BESS NWA program as opex earns nothing.
      →Data center and large load growth is financially beneficial: new large customers expand the rate base, justify major capital programs, and increase volumetric revenue.
      →Performance-based ratemaking (PBR) in NY, CA, and HI attempts to link earnings to outcomes rather than capital spending. Early results are mixed.
      IOUs are incentivized to maximize capital investment in distribution upgrades under cost-of-service regulation. NWAs are financially disadvantageous unless regulators explicitly allow return on NWA investments or implement PBR mechanisms.
    
    
      Municipal utility (muni / POU)
      Not-for-profit; ~30% of US customers via majority of utilities by count
      Revenue model
      →Non-profit structure. Revenue reinvested in system or returned via rate reductions. No shareholder return requirement.
      →Capital financed through municipal revenue bonds at 4–5% interest — lower cost of capital than IOUs (~7–8% WACC blended).
      Incentive implications
      →No structural bias toward capital over NWAs. A $10M NWA that avoids a $15M feeder upgrade saves ratepayers $5M with no earnings impact.
      →Directly accountable to customers who are voters and board members. Rate increases are politically visible, creating strong incentives to minimize costs.
      →Resource-constrained. Smaller engineering teams and less analytical capacity may lead to infrastructure-first decisions by default rather than incentive.
      Munis have better structural incentive alignment with customers on NWA adoption, but face capacity constraints. The gap is institutional capability, not financial incentive.
    
  
  Practical implication: an IOU's planning team needs NWA analysis packaged as a rate case–ready avoided cost document that a PUC will accept. A muni's board needs a rate impact table showing upgrade cost vs. NWA program cost per ratepayer per year. Same calculation, different presentation layer.

title: Distribution Grid Analysis: A Primer
section: How Outputs Translate to Planning Decisions and Stakeholder Actions
text:
How Outputs Translate to Planning Decisions and Stakeholder Actions
Raw simulation outputs — voltage profiles, thermal loading, hosting capacity estimates — only become useful when translated into the terms each stakeholder depends on. Planners need violation timelines tied to capital schedules; regulators need investment justification; developers need interconnection feasibility; and customers need rate and reliability projections. That translation is largely non-standardized, and the widget below maps both the three impact domains and where the pipeline breaks down by stakeholder group.

Stakeholder
What they need
How outputs are translated today
Gap




Distribution planners
Which feeders to upgrade, when, at what cost
Direct use of load flow, HCA, and fault analysis outputs; violation counts by year by scenario
No standard output format; feeder-level results rarely integrated with capital planning models automatically


State regulators (PUCs)
Are proposed investments justified? Are alternatives considered? Are costs allocated fairly?
Utility rate case filings presenting study results selectively; intervenor expert witnesses challenge assumptions
In ~30 states, regulators do not receive distribution system plans before rate cases; planning assumptions are not routinely disclosed


Large load customers

Interconnection study results: thermal headroom, upgrade requirements, cost estimate
Study timelines of 3–9 months, lack of utility standardization, and no national process for distribution-level large loads


DER developers
Can I interconnect? Where is capacity? What will upgrades cost?
Hosting capacity maps; interconnection study if above simplified threshold
HCA maps often stale; maps don't communicate upgrade cost or feasibility to reach next MW of headroom


EV charging developers
Will my EVSE installation require feeder upgrades? At whose cost?
Utility load study if above site service threshold; no federal standard exists
No mandatory interconnection study below MW threshold; cumulative feeder impact from many EVSE installations is not assessed


Community / EJ advocates
Are investments reaching disadvantaged communities? Are rate impacts equitable?
Not systematically provided; requires bespoke spatial analysis linking feeder maps, census data, and rate impact models
No standardized pipeline from distribution study outputs to equity metrics; CPUC D.24-10-030 (Oct 2024) begins to mandate this in CA only


Residential customers
Will my rates change? Will reliability improve?
Rate case summary documents; SAIDI/SAIFI reporting
Translation from engineering metrics to customer outcomes is not standardized anywhere








The most immediate opportunities lie in extending the integrated distribution planning frameworks developed in New York, California, and Illinois — requiring public disclosure of system plans, combined multi-technology study standards, and systematic NWA evaluation — to the rest of the US, roughly 30 states where no such requirements currently exist. Establishing a federal research agenda to fill the empirical gap in multi-stressor feeder analysis would also valuable.

title: Curtailment: Can we avoid it?
text:
NoteKey takeaways




Curtailment is growing in every major grid, even as storage expands
Most of California’s curtailment is a transmission problem, additional batteries likely won’t improve the situation
Sites where large loads co-locate with intermittent generators (wind, solar) are facing regulatory challenges
Targeted transmission investment (Texas CREZ, Germany’s SuedLink) is the only solution proven to work at scale
My interest in analyzing curtailment data came from my analysis of battery storage. Since higher curtailment means a bigger opportunity for batteries, I wanted to understand whether higher deployment of battery storage has had any measurable impact on curtailment. The short answer is yes, but it is not yet clearly visible in the data.
Curtailment occurs due to two distinct reasons. Local curtailment (also called congestion or reliability curtailment) occurs when a specific transmission line or substation is at its physical limit and interconnected generation cannot be exported regardless of conditions elsewhere on the grid. System curtailment (also called economic curtailment) occurs when total supply across the region exceeds consumer demand, driving wholesale prices to zero or below. System curtailment can be absorbed by batteries anywhere on the grid, while local curtailment needs solutions in the specific constrained area — co-located storage, substation upgrades, or more transmission capacity. Batteries respond primarily to price signals, but local curtailment is not necessarily price-driven. This is why the location where storage is sited and how markets price congestion determine whether storage can actually reduce curtailment.

title: Curtailment: Can we avoid it?

The geographic specificity of curtailment
Figure 4 shows this breakdown for California, where local curtailment has continued growing even as battery capacity has expanded rapidly.

  Figure 4: CAISO Annual Curtailment by Type — Local vs. System
  Annual wind and solar curtailment (GWh), 2022–2025. System curtailment = grid-wide overgeneration, absorbable by batteries anywhere. Local curtailment = transmission-constrained; only co-located or same-zone storage helps. Source: CAISO Production and Curtailments reports (Local/System breakdown available from 2022).
202220232024202501,0002,000LocalSystemAnnual Curtailment(GWh)

In CAISO local (transmission-constrained) curtailment accounted for roughly 78–92% of total curtailed energy in 2022–2025, while system-wide overgeneration curtailment fell.6 ERCOT does not publish an equivalent formal split, but Potomac Economics’ 2024 State of the Market analysis estimated that approximately 74% of curtailment events were driven by unpriced congestion signals, with the West Texas Export constraint alone responsible for roughly a fifth of all curtailed renewable energy.78 Germany’s Redispatch 2.0 mechanism is by definition a congestion-management tool, meaning its reported curtailment volumes are inherently local rather than system-wide.910
Other analysts have mapped where curtailment concentrates spatially, using LMP price spreads and publicly available interconnection and deliverability data.11 The examples below show this for CAISO and ERCOT.12131415

title: Curtailment: Can we avoid it?
text:
Some solutions
Texas SB 6, passed in 2025, created the first formal framework for co-location in ERCOT — pairing large electricity consumers directly with generators to bypass the transmission grid — and there are already 28+ projects in the queue.1617 They span a wide range: AI data centers paired with wind farms, gas plants and nuclear operators.181920 Not all of these deals help with curtailment. Only pairing with intermittent generators — wind and solar — puts a load behind the meter where renewable energy would otherwise go to waste. Yet SB 6’s regulatory process has been hardest on the wind and solar pairings. The projects most likely to reduce curtailment are the ones facing the most friction to get built — a real missed opportunity.21
The one thing proven to work at scale is building transmission. CREZ was launched after ERCOT identified that West Texas held some of the best wind resources in the country but had no high-voltage path to move that power to Gulf Coast load centers. Building it required an act of the Texas legislature in 2005 and took nearly a decade to complete. It was opposed by fossil fuel generators who didn’t want cheap wind competing with their plants, industrial consumers who resented sharing the cost, and landowners who fought the routes across their land — disputes that added hundreds of miles to the original plan and pushed the final bill from an initial estimate of $4.9 billion to $6.8 billion. This was funded by all ERCOT ratepayers at around $4 per month on their electricity bills. Around 3,600 miles of new high-voltage lines were completed by 2013, and curtailment fell from 17% of available wind output in 2009 to under 3% by 2014.22 Germany is doing the same at larger scale with the SuedLink and SuedOstLink HVDC lines — around 1,280 km of underground cable at 2 GW each, connecting surplus northern wind to industrial demand in Bavaria and Baden-Württemberg, with commissioning targeted for 2028–2029 at a combined cost of around €10 billion.23
FERC Order 1920, issued in May 2024 under the title Building for the Future Through Electric Regional Transmission Planning and Cost Allocation, addresses a structural flaw in how the US grid has historically been planned: new transmission is largely built reactively, through fragmented utility-by-utility processes or only when individual generators needed a connection. This rule requires regional transmission operators to develop 20-year forward-looking plans, updated every five years, across multiple scenarios that account for projected renewable growth, rising demand, and extreme weather. Proposed projects must pass a benefit-cost test before selection.24 Another solution which makes capacity immediately available is dynamic line rating using real-time temperature and wind data to calculate how much more current existing conductors can safely carry, typically unlocking 10–40% more capacity without new construction.2526
Proactive transmission planning, as Texas demonstrated, is fraught with difficulties. But it pays off — the end result is cheaper electricity.






NoteData and methods





CAISO Production and Curtailments reports: Five-minute interval curtailment data by fuel type (solar, wind) and reason (Local, System), downloaded from the CAISO OASIS public portal.27 Local curtailment occurs when a transmission bottleneck limits export from a specific area; System curtailment reflects grid-wide overgeneration. Curtailment energy in GWh is computed as MW × (5/60) / 1,000, summed over all intervals. Data covers January 2018 through May 2025.
EIA Form 860M: Monthly generator-level data, April 2026 snapshot.28 Battery storage capacity (GWh) for CAISO and ERCOT is computed from the Nameplate Energy Capacity (MWh) field, filtered to Technology == "Batteries" and the relevant balancing authority codes (CISO, ERCO). Cumulative installed capacity by year is derived by summing nameplate energy for all units with an operating year on or before each calendar year.
ERCOT 60-Day SCED Disclosure (NP3-965-ER): Hourly dispatch data for all ERCOT resources, covering January 2020 through December 2025. Downloaded via the ERCOT Public API. Curtailment is computed as the gap between a resource’s high sustainable limit (HSL) and its telemetered net output, aggregated by fuel type (PVGR = solar, WIND = wind). As discussed in the article, this method undercounts total curtailment by approximately 2.7× relative to settlement-metered totals because economic self-curtailment — generators reducing their HSL submission during negative-price intervals — does not appear as curtailment in SCED snapshots.29
ERCOT actual vs. potential generation (NP4-737-CD, NP4-732-CD): System-wide potential generation (PVGRPP_SYSTEM_WIDE, WGRPP_SYSTEM_WIDE) versus actual generation, downloaded via the ERCOT Public API for 2024. Used as an independent cross-check on SCED-derived curtailment estimates. Yields a similar undercount (2,970 GWh for 2024) for the same structural reasons as the SCED method.
S&P Global Energy Horizons / 451 Research: Settlement-metered ERCOT curtailment totals by fuel type, from the March 2026 North American Renewable Curtailment Report. Used as the primary magnitude reference for ERCOT annual curtailment (2020–2025). These figures use settlement-quality metering data not available in public ERCOT disclosures; the ratio of S&P totals to SCED-derived totals implies a 2.7× undercount in the public data for 2024.
Germany — BNetzA Redispatch 2.0: Annual curtailment totals from the Federal Network Agency’s grid management monitoring. Figures for 2024 (9,335 GWh) are from the BNetzA annual grid management report published April 2025,30 covering solar, onshore wind, and offshore wind separately. The 2025 figure (~9,380 GWh) is from SMARD/BNetzA data published May 2026, with the same three-way breakdown — essentially flat versus 2024 despite a 22% drop in compensation costs, since curtailment shifted from costlier offshore wind toward cheaper solar.31 The 2023 figure (~10,400 GWh) is derived by back-calculating from BNetzA-published year-over-year percentage changes for each fuel type. The 2022 figure (~8,000 GWh) is from Synertics analysis of BNetzA data. Pre-2022 figures use the old §14 EEG EinsMan series (TSO-level only). The Redispatch 2.0 framework, in effect from October 2021, extended reporting to include DSO-level curtailment; the two series are not directly comparable.
Germany — BNetzA Marktstammdatenregister (MaStR): Registry of grid-connected generation and storage units. Utility-scale battery capacity (Großbatteriespeicher, ≥1 MW) is drawn from MaStR-derived figures: end-2022 confirmed by Figgener et al. (arxiv:2203.06762); end-2023 by BVES Branchenanalyse 2024; end-2025 by Modo Energy (February 2026) and BNetzA MaStR. Excludes approximately 3 million residential home batteries tracked separately in Energy-Charts total figures.
Australia — AEMO Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED): Quarterly curtailment reports for the National Electricity Market, Q1 2020 through Q4 2025. Includes both network curtailment (grid operator orders) and economic offloading (generator self-curtailment during low or negative price periods). Does not include Western Australia (SWIS). 2024 and 2025 annual figures are cross-checked against WattClarity’s full-year aggregation of AEMO data (4.3 TWh and 7.2 TWh respectively); earlier years are summed from quarterly reports.32
Grid Status: Open-source Python library and web interface (gridstatus.io) used to access locational marginal price data across CAISO, ERCOT, and other ISOs for congestion analysis. LMP spreads between generation nodes and load centers are used as an indicator of transmission constraint severity.33

title: Energy and Electrification in U.S. Agriculture
text:
The U.S. is one of the largest exporters of agricultural products in the world. In 2024, it ranked first globally with $176 billion in agricultural exports to 189 countries, with nearly half going to just three destinations: Mexico ($30.3B), Canada ($28.4B), and China ($24.7B). Despite the high value of this sector — which represents about 20% of total U.S. agricultural output — agriculture’s direct contribution to GDP remains slightly below 1%, making it a relatively small component of the broader U.S. economy.
The US agricultural sector manages 880 million acres of farmland—39% of total US land area—with cropland alone covering 390 million acres (17% of total US land) 1. This massive land base supports a diverse agricultural economy ranging from irrigated specialty crops in California to corn and soybean production in the Midwest to cattle ranching across the Great Plains.
From an energy perspective, understanding direct on-farm energy consumption is critical because these are the operations farmers directly control and where electrification opportunities are most immediate and actionable.

title: Duration and Dispatch: Comparing Battery Storage Across CAISO and ERCOT
text:
This article was inspired by an image I saw a few weeks ago1, comparing the installed capacity of battery storage in the CAISO and ERCOT markets. I was intrigued by the rapid growth of battery storage in the last few years and decided to see if I could reproduce the figure from publicly available data.
Figure 1a below uses EIA-860M2 data reported as of April 2026. The net summer capacity of battery storage was used to calculate the cumulative battery storage capacity by year. The planned capacity is shown as two separate series, one filtered to reflect capacity that is more certain to come online (regulatory approval received and/or construction started), and one with the full planned total. The figure shows that both markets had roughly 16 GW of cumulative battery storage capacity by early 2026, but the speculative pipeline in ERCOT is much larger, with the planned capacity nearly double CAISO’s capacity by 2030. One thing to note — the original figure has an ambiguous x-axis, failing to show that the large growth is expected by 2030, rather than in 2025. While ERCOT’s capacity is growing faster, the gap between the filtered and total planned capacity suggests significant uncertainty.
Figure 1b shows the same fleet in terms of nameplate energy capacity (GWh). Despite both markets reaching similar installed power capacity (~16 GW), California’s batteries average roughly 3.4 hours of storage duration compared to ~1.6 hours in Texas, meaning CAISO holds more than twice the stored energy per unit of rated power — a structural difference in how each market has deployed storage.

title: Duration and Dispatch: Comparing Battery Storage Across CAISO and ERCOT
text:
Design and Dispatch
Next, I processed the data in order to identify the following characteristics which in turn reveal the operational models for battery storage in the two markets:

Duration of battery storage
Co-located vs standalone capacity
Co-located vs standalone capacity by duration
Seasonal dispatch patterns and nodal price


Duration of battery storage
Duration of the battery by site was calculated for each individual generator (generator id in the data) by dividing the energy capacity by the capacity of the unit. This was then categorized into bins. Figure 2 shows the cumulative power capacity for each duration category and year by state.
In California, the median battery storage duration is 4 hours while in Texas it is around 2 hours. The most plausible reason for this difference is California’s Resource Adequacy (RA) program which requires Load-Serving Entities (utilities, Community Choice Aggregators, and retail sellers) to procure and demonstrate sufficient generating capacity to cover their customers’ peak load plus a 15% planning reserve margin. LSEs must show this capacity is contracted and deliverable. CPUC and CAISO’s requirement is that a storage resource must demonstrate 4 hours of discharge duration at rated capacity to receive full RA capacity credit. Shorter-duration storage receives proportionally reduced credit — roughly 50% credit for 2-hour storage — making the incremental cost of going from 2 to 4 hours almost always worth it for the RA market.3,4,5
Texas has no equivalent requirement. Batteries earn revenue by trading energy and the duration that maximises revenue is what gets deployed. This turns out to be around two hours, short enough to allow multiple charge-discharge cycles across the daily price swing and long enough to capture the main arbitrage window.6,7

Co-located vs standalone
Co-location was derived from the EIA-860M data using Plant ID matching: a battery is classified as co-located if its Plant ID also appears as the Plant ID of a solar or wind generator within the same grid area. This is a lower-bound estimate — projects registered under different Plant IDs at the same site will appear as standalone (co-located counts are therefore likely understated, particularly in Texas where large integrated projects sometimes use separate registrations).

California has a larger share of batteries paired with solar generators while Texas has predominantly standalone battery storage, shown in Figure 3. The most plausible reasons for this difference are incentive structures. Before the Inflation Reduction Act (August 2022), battery storage could only qualify for the federal Investment Tax Credit (ITC) if it was charged exclusively from an on-site solar facility. Standalone batteries charged from the grid did not qualify. Therefore many projects were paired with solar specifically to access the 30% ITC. California’s Self-Generation Incentive Program (SGIP) had a dedicated large-scale storage budget.8 Large-scale projects that could demonstrate 75%+ charging from on-site solar qualified for the higher “paired renewable” incentive tier ($2.00/W base + $2.50/W resiliency adder = $4.50/W), versus the plain storage rate. This created an incremental financial case for co-locating solar and storage, particularly during 2019–2022 when the large-scale budget was still available.


Co-location × Duration
Figure 4 below shows the share of installed capacity by duration class within each co-location type. In CAISO, standalone batteries concentrate heavily at 3–4 hours (81%), while co-located solar batteries show a wider spread — 65% at 3–4hr, 28% at ≤2hr, and a small ≥5hr tail.
Standalone batteries skew toward longer durations because as discussed earlier, they are built primarily for Resource Adequacy and CPUC’s RA program requires 4 hours of discharge at rated capacity for full NQC credit.
Batteries co-located with solar have a wider spread. The higher ≤2hr share is likely due to many pre-IRA co-located batteries (2019–2022) built primarily to access the 30% ITC rather than to optimise for RA, with duration set by developer economics.

Seasonal dispatch patterns and nodal price
I wanted to understand the relationship between storage dispatch and curtailment to see if any conclusions could be drawn about higher storage capacity being able to reduce curtailment. However, it appears that storage is still playing catch-up to solar deployment in California, and I decided to save that for a more detailed analysis in a separate article. Here, I’ve pivoted to showing average nodal energy prices alongside charging and discharging energy by season for the two markets. The intention is primarily to identify and confirm that battery operation is optimized around wholesale market prices. A few things are confirmed by these data patterns and the information on co-location presented earlier:

In CAISO, co-located batteries which were installed under ITC can only charge from their paired solar/wind generation. In practice this means co-located batteries cannot participate in arbitrage the same way as standalone batteries.
Also, in CAISO, most battery projects get their primary source of revenue from resource adequacy contracts9 while in ERCOT they rely more heavily on energy arbitrage and ancillary services.10
Both markets show a strong midday charging signal driven by solar generation suppressing prices, with discharge concentrated in the evening demand peak.

Data and Methods
EIA-860M: Monthly generator-level data from EIA Form 860M, April 2026 snapshot.13 Battery units are identified by Technology == "Batteries". Duration is computed as Nameplate Energy Capacity (MWh) ÷ Net Summer Capacity (MW). Co-location type is inferred by matching Plant ID between battery and solar/wind generators within the same balancing authority. Both duration and co-location are analytical constructs derived from the data; neither is a field that EIA publishes directly.
CAISO fuel mix: Five-minute interval net generation by fuel type, 2018–2025, downloaded via the gridstatus.io API14 (underlying source: CAISO OASIS).15 Battery storage is reported as a signed net value (positive = discharging, negative = charging).
CAISO SP15 LMP: Day-ahead locational marginal prices at the SP15 hub, 2024–2025, downloaded via the gridstatus.io API.
ERCOT battery dispatch: Hourly ESR charge and discharge data, December 2023 to present, downloaded via the gridstatus.io API.16
ERCOT DAM Settlement Point Prices: Annual Excel files containing hourly day-ahead settlement point prices for ERCOT hubs. HB_NORTH used as the reference hub price. 2024–2025 annual files.17

title: What Do Data Center Load Profiles Look Like?
text:
The answer is both simple and complex. While load profiles are typically flat, the shape depends on several factors: the type of data center, the mix of workloads it supports, and its infrastructure design.
The widget below summarizes key factors that influence load profile characteristics.
Ideally, metered data would allow for better quantification of load shapes and help refine existing models. But multiple barriers stand in the way. First, most utilities don’t require load profile submissions during interconnection making baseline data scarce. Second, even when data exists, the workloads of any specific data center are likely to evolve continuously, both in the short term and long term with technology adoption and shifting user demand. The ESIG report on large load forecasting1 provides additonal details on issues and methods to forecast large loads. In the absence of metered data, tools such as Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory’s Shape Maker (GitHub) can generate synthetic hourly load profiles based on data center characteristics.
This is an evolving area of interest as data centers become a larger share of electricity demand — better quantifying these profiles will be essential for planning and forecasting.

title: Exploring EIA 930: The Shape of U.S. Electricity Demand
text: I’ve been spending some time diving into EIA 930 data,1,2 with the goal of general exploratory analysis of this large dataset. The analysis revealed several interesting data patterns, but explaining these in a reasonable and verifiable manner was the major challenge. In this article, I present my analysis in a sequential format, however, the actual workflow was largely exploratory and required a lot of trial-and-error until I was satisfied with the content. Beyond scanning and statistical approaches, I’ve also reproduced some ‘storylines’ at the end of the article that have already been previously identified in other publications or articles.

title: Exploring EIA 930: The Shape of U.S. Electricity Demand
text:
Note on data exploration
Both the approaches to storytelling from data showcased in this article have pros and cons. The broad scan approach takes a long time and can lead to nowhere, but the benefit is that you understand the data quality, stuctuture and its quirks after a while. Selecting specific subsets of the data to support a storyline is a quicker and more effective approach, but this is susceptible to bias and there is a risk of ignoring alternative storyline. Data selection is an art, and having a hypotheses or domain knowledge is extremely important when working with big data, simply because a lot of large datasets rarely lend themselves to easy classification. I will also note that LLMs tend to suggest very complex metrics for data analysis that just dont work well with aggregated or noisy data. In this analysis I pivoted to mainly looking at outliers because other load duration curve (LDC) based metrics required deeper contextual knowledge.

title: Exploring EIA 930: The Shape of U.S. Electricity Demand
text:
Data
Source: EIA Cleaned Hourly Electricity Demand Data. The dataset applies a cleaning algorithm to EIA-930 hourly demand reports, flagging and imputing anomalous values using a Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) technique.
Releases used: - Release 1 (release_2020_Oct): July 2, 2015 to July 1, 2020 — 54 BAs and 13 regions - Release 2 (release_2025_Jan_include_subregions): January 1, 2020 to December 31, 2024 — 119 BA subregion files
Stitching: Where both releases cover the same year, Release 1 data is used through December 31, 2019; Release 2 begins January 1, 2020. The six-month overlap (Jan–Jul 2020) is not double-counted.
Full-year filter: Years with fewer than 95% of expected hours (8,760 for standard years, 8,784 for leap years) are excluded from LDC and boundary metric calculations. This affects 2015 (partial year, data begins July 2) in some analyses.
Weather normalization: BA-scale comparisons use weather-normalized demand (log-OLS regression of monthly MWh on HDD, CDD, their squares, month fixed effects, and a year trend; the resulting coefficients are used to rescale each hour to what demand would have been under climatologically normal weather). The weather normals are computed as the 2016–2024 monthly mean HDD/CDD per entity. For regions and larger BAs — which have data from both releases back to 2015 — this is a genuine nine-year average. For BA subregions, which only appear in Release 2 (from 2020), the effective baseline is the 2020–2024 mean. The weather correction for subregions carries more uncertainty than for the larger entities.
Timestamps: EIA-930 timestamps are stored in UTC. For diurnal analyses, timestamps are converted to local prevailing time per time zone before extracting hour, month, and year.
Analysis notebook: The figures in this article were produced using eia_explore.ipynb, available in the same repository. The notebook covers the demand change distribution, BA annual demand profiles, load shape spread metrics, and differential heatmaps. The weather normalization pipeline (scripts 01–05) is in LDD/weather_norm/.

title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
text:
What is a heat pump, and why is it different from an AC?
A heat pump is a device that transfers heat using a small amount of electricity to run a compression cycle. It doesn’t create heat the way a furnace or boiler does — it utilizes heat that already exists in the outdoor air, the ground, or a body of water. That distinction allows heat pumps to provide both heating and cooling.
Mechanically, a heat pump is essentially an air conditioner that can run in reverse. Both share a common architecture: compressor, refrigerant loop, indoor and outdoor coils, and fans. However, a heat pump incorporates specific elements to enable reverse-cycle operation, high efficiency, and reliable cold-weather performance. These include a four-way reversing valve to switch refrigerant flow direction, an inverter-driven variable-speed compressor, modern lower-GWP refrigerants (such as R-32 or R-454B), and advanced defrost controls with low-temperature starting capabilities.
While heat pumps in cooling mode are not necessarily more efficient than ACs, their value comes from providing both heating and cooling and the ability to operate at partial loads. Heat pumps excel at operating efficiently in moderate climates and offer flexible form factors like single-room mini-splits. They are not designed for instant high-temperature output; rather, they provide a more consistent, steady delivery of heating or cooling.

title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
text:
What is a heat pump, and why is it different from an AC?
A heat pump is a device that transfers heat using a small amount of electricity to run a compression cycle. It doesn’t create heat the way a furnace or boiler does — it utilizes heat that already exists in the outdoor air, the ground, or a body of water. That distinction allows heat pumps to provide both heating and cooling.
Mechanically, a heat pump is essentially an air conditioner that can run in reverse. Both share a common architecture: compressor, refrigerant loop, indoor and outdoor coils, and fans. However, a heat pump incorporates specific elements to enable reverse-cycle operation, high efficiency, and reliable cold-weather performance. These include a four-way reversing valve to switch refrigerant flow direction, an inverter-driven variable-speed compressor, modern lower-GWP refrigerants (such as R-32 or R-454B), and advanced defrost controls with low-temperature starting capabilities.
While heat pumps in cooling mode are not necessarily more efficient than ACs, their value comes from providing both heating and cooling and the ability to operate at partial loads. Heat pumps excel at operating efficiently in moderate climates and offer flexible form factors like single-room mini-splits. title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
section: Part 2: History of heat pump deployment
text:
Part 2: History of heat pump deployment
Air-source heat pumps entered the mass market in the 1950s with manufacturers like GE and Carrier introducing central ducted systems, followed by the development of “mini-split” heat pumps in Japan. The 1973 oil crisis served as a global catalyst for adoption, doubling the technology’s U.S. footprint. A critical technical turning point occurred in 1980 with the Japanese invention of the inverter-driven compressor, transitioning systems from inefficient cycling operation to the variable-speed modulation seen today.
In the early 2000s, air-source heat pumps (ASHPs) were primarily installed to provide cooling. They were standard single-stage split systems whose coefficient of performance degraded steadily as outdoor temperature dropped, hitting a balance point typically between 25–35°F, where electric resistance “strip heat” had to take over. Adoption clustered in mild climates: the U.S. Southeast, southern China, and Mediterranean Europe. In those markets, a heat pump was mostly an upgrade path from electric resistance heat.
By 2010, cold-climate engineering matured and variable-speed inverter compressors, vapor injection, and advanced refrigerants became the standard. Mitsubishi’s Hyper-Heating Inverter (H2i) line, along with comparable systems from Daikin and Fujitsu, established that air-source units could deliver useful heat at sub-zero temperatures. The launch of the Northeast Energy Efficiency Partnership (NEEP) Cold Climate ASHP Specification in 2015 provided the first rigorous, third-party benchmark for low-temperature performance. This allowed utility programs to transition away from simple rebates and toward high-performance incentives. In China, the massive “Coal-to-Electricity” campaign retrofitted millions of rural homes from coal stoves to heat pumps. This campaign provided the manufacturing volumes that lowered global hardware costs.
By the early 2020s, a convergence of high natural gas prices and aggressive policy shifted the heat pump from an option to the “incumbent” heating technology. A major milestone was reached in 2022 when heat pumps officially surpassed gas furnaces in U.S. annual unit shipments—a lead that grew to 12% by 2025. As we look toward 2030, the conversation has moved beyond mere appliance efficiency toward the heat pump’s role as a vital grid asset.

title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
text:
Part 3: Market Deep-Dives
The specific type of heat pump technology in a particular country or region is heavily influenced by the existing technology that is being replaced. The cost of conversion is the primary influencing factor in decision-making and is more important than lifetime cost of operation.

Japan and South Korea: The efficiency leaders
Japan’s residential heat pump penetration is nearly universal. It is anchored by the “Eco Cute,” a CO2 (R744) heat pump water heater introduced in 2001 that became the national standard. Conversion costs are minimal because cooling infrastructure is already heat-pump-based. Kerosene heaters are standalone room appliances, meaning there is no central distribution to abandon. “Upgrading” typically means replacing aging mini-split heads at a cost of $1,500–$3,000 per zone. Japan represents the lowest-friction case globally because it never built the central distribution infrastructure that other nations must now decommission.
South Korea views building electrification as a national security priority, importing over 90% of its primary energy. Zero Energy Building (ZEB) certifications and subsidies anchor the policy stack. The ondol radiant floor is the dominant existing technology. Air-to-water heat pumps can drive these floor loops directly if they were sized for low-temp operation (common in modern construction; rarer in older homes). Where the conversion works, homeowners replace only the boiler for roughly $8k–$15k. Where ondol cannot accept low-temp water, the pragmatic path involves keeping the gas boiler and adding mini-splits for cooling and shoulder-season heating.


European Union: The hydronic shift
Western and Central Europe lead in air-to-water systems, integrated into existing radiator and underfloor networks. REPowerEU — the EU’s response to dependence on Russian gas — accelerated the pivot, and France, Germany, and Italy have all moved to phase out new fossil boiler installations in some form. Norway, Sweden, and Finland hold the highest heat pump penetration rates in the world; ground-source heat pumps are the dominant technology in these countries.
Hydronic radiators are the most common in Germany, making air-to-water monobloc heat pumps the natural fit. Installation costs range from €25k–€40k, with Bundesförderung für effiziente Gebäude (BEG) programs covering 30–70% of the cost. The hidden friction is radiator sizing: low-temperature heat pump operation (45–55°C) requires larger surface areas than gas boilers (70–80°C). Many homes require radiator upgrades or weatherization first, and oil tank removal is mandatory under German regulations.
In France, the market is split between the 30% of households on electric wall panel heaters and the rest in the gas/oil hydronic segment. For those on electric wall panel heaters, the path is ductless mini-splits (€8k–€18k). The hydronic segment mirrors Germany, with air-to-water systems costing €15k–€30k, after considering the MaPrimeRénov’ subsidy structure.
District heating is common in Nordic countries, and disconnecting from district heat is rarely allowed or economic. For the non-DH segment, hydronic distribution typically already exists, and air-to-water heat pumps fit cleanly. Norway is unique; high mini-split adoption came from homes with electric baseboards simply adding ductless units (NOK 30k–80k per zone). Sweden and Finland have more existing hydronic and ground-source infrastructure, so air-to-water and ground-source remain dominant retrofit choices.
United Kingdom: The UK serves as a cautionary tale where subsidies have not yet met targets. Adoption is hampered by a poor electricity-to-gas price ratio, a thin installer pipeline, and an inefficient housing stock. Most UK radiators are sized for high-temperature combi boilers (~70°C); transitioning to a heat pump often requires new radiators, the addition of a hot water cylinder, and consumer unit upgrades. Net costs after the £7,500 BUS grant often land at £8k–£14k, compared to just £2.5k–£4k for a like-for-like boiler replacement.


China: The volume engine
China is the world’s largest heat pump market and manufacturer. While domestic sales have plateaued due to macroeconomic softness, exports continue to grow. In Southern China, “conversion” is often just a usage shift toward winter heating with existing cooling units. In Northern rural areas, Coal-to-Electricity conversions are new installations of subsidized mini-splits (RMB 8k–20k), benefiting from the fact that these homes mostly skipped the central-distribution stage of HVAC development.


United States: The ducted transformation
The U.S. is unique in its reliance on air-to-air ducted systems—a legacy of the central AC era. Cold-climate inverter performance has unlocked the Northeast and Midwest, supported by IRA tax credits (25C and HEEHRA). As of late 2024, nine states had pledged that heat pumps would account for at least 65% of residential heating-and-cooling sales by 2030.
The conversion cost story in the Northeast splits sharply by housing stock. Newer suburban homes with gas furnaces and central AC are the easiest cases with a ducted central ASHP swap costing $8k–$15k. Existing ductwork is generally reusable, though heat pumps move roughly 20–30% more airflow per BTU than gas furnaces and older systems often need duct modifications or static pressure adjustments ($1–5k). Older urban housing with oil-fired radiators is much harder; conversion to mini-split heat pumps run $15k–$25k for whole-home multi-zone systems and often require oil tank removal and electric panel upgrades ($2k–$4k).
Most homes in the Midwest are forced-air with gas furnace plus central AC sharing ductwork — the easiest case to swap with ASHP. Replacement runs $10–18k for a hybrid dual-fuel setup that keeps the gas furnace as backup. This sidesteps cold-snap reliability concerns and the high cost of full gas decommissioning.
The Southeast is the cheapest conversion market in the US because the existing system is usually a central electric strip heat package or a low-efficiency heat pump sharing ductwork with central AC. ASHP replacement runs $8–14k, ductwork is fully reusable, no fuel infrastructure to decommission, no panel upgrade typically needed, and the homeowner is already paying electric heating bills so the operational case is straightforward.
In the Mountain West and Plains, gas furnace plus evaporative cooler is the regionally distinctive setup. The furnace ductwork is reusable for a ducted ASHP swap ($10–16k), but the evaporative cooler — typically roof-mounted or window-mounted — is abandoned in place or removed ($500–1,500 for removal and roof patching).
In the Pacific Northwest, a surge in cooling demand after the 2021 heat dome transformed this market. Most retrofits are ductless mini-splits added to homes with electric baseboard heat. Single-zone installs run $4–8k, multi-zone systems $12–25k. Due to this low marginal cost of conversion, this region has been a leader on heat pump deployment per capita despite a small starting installed base.
Alaska is the highest-friction market. Oil-fired hydronic systems, remote labor, and shipping costs push conversion economics over $30k, concentrating deployment in milder coastal areas.

title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
text:
Part 4: The Path Forward

1. Product Development and contractor capacity
Window heat pumps have proven the concept, but currently serve only a small slice of the 50 million room AC units in the U.S. We need products that fit casement and slider windows (unlocking 8–12 million households), higher-capacity units for studio apartments (14,000–18,000 BTU), and cold-climate packaged terminal heat pumps for Northeast multifamily buildings. These segments are constrained by physical fit, not financing. Targeted challenges—similar to the $263 million NYCHA Clean Heat for All Challenge—could produce these products within 3–5 years.
Simultaneously, the 2025 A2L refrigerant transition has made contractor capacity the true deployment ceiling. Many smaller installers and contractors are dropping out rather than retooling for spark-resistant hardware and updated EPA Section 608 certifications. Workforce development—apprenticeships and training subsidies—is currently underweighted. The other side of the problem is intervention design: the highest-leverage moment for adoption is the AC failure, when a homeowner is making a 48-hour replacement decision. Midstream programs that move incentives upstream to distributors and contractors (so a heat pump is cheaper than a comparable AC at wholesale) are far more effective than downstream rebates.


2. VPP Integration
We are moving from “appliance efficiency” to “system-level value.” The U.S. grid needs 200 GW of new peak capacity by 2030. Aggregating smart heat pumps into Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) could meet 10–20% of that need at a 40–60% lower cost than new gas peakers. With federal 25C tax credits having expired at the end of 2025, grid services revenue is the most credible candidate to fill the funding gap. VPP enrollment can provide homeowners with $200–$500 in annual revenue, significantly improving the economics of a conversion. As utilities like Georgia Power build new gas plants to meet data center demand, replacing aging heat pumps with high-efficiency, VPP-ready units provides a vital hedge. Whether load forecasts are accurate or inflated, efficiency gains accrue to the customer and reduce the risk of stranded utility assets.

title: Heat Pumps for the Residential Sector: Drivers of Adoption
text:
Summary
Heat pumps are a proven, cost-competitive technology that has outsold gas furnaces in the U.S. since 2022. Expanding the product portfolio for urban housing, building the contractor workforce, and unlocking the value of heat pumps as flexible grid assets are key to maintaining momentum through 2030 and beyond.
